description:
The Passport Program (formerly Passport Canada) began using facial
recognition technology in 2010 to support identity authentication, help detect
fraud, and prevent passport and travel document issuance to ineligible
applicants.
Facial recognition technology is a tool that detects and analyzes the facial
features of a person’s face for the purpose of assisting in the identification
and verification of an individual’s identity. A facial recognition system converts
facial images into facial biometric templates that may be stored within the
system and used for future comparison. The system can compare a facial
biometric against a collection of other facial biometrics resulting in a list of
candidates ranked by a computer evaluated similarity score; this is called a
one-to-many (1:N) identification. It can also conduct a verification by
comparing a facial biometric to another facial biometric in the same claimed
identity; this is called a one-to-one (1:1) verification.
For passport purposes, three comparisons are performed. First, all adult
applicant facial biometrics are compared to a database of biometric
templates from past passport applicants to determine if the person has
previously applied for a passport and to help detect passport and travel
document related fraud. Similarly all adult applicants are compared to a
collection of templates of individuals whose entitlement to a Canadian
passport or travel document warrants additional review (e.g. individuals who
have previously misrepresented their identity). Finally, for returning applicants,
the Passport Program's facial recognition system compares the applicant's
facial biometric to previous templates in the same identity (i.e. previous
passport records) to verify they are the same person.
qa_section_text:
Section 3.1: Impact Questions and Answers
Reasons for Automation
1. What is motivating your team to introduce automation into this decision-making process?
(Check all that apply)
Improve overall quality of decisions
Lower transaction costs of an existing program
The system is performing tasks that humans could not accomplish in a
reasonable period of time
Use innovative approaches
2. What client needs will the system address and how will this system meet them? If possible,
describe how client needs have been identified.
Clients want a secure, globally respected travel document that will facilitate
domestic and international travel. Canadian passport holders enjoy visa-free
access to most countries due, in part, to Canada's reputation of having strong
control measures, like the use of facial recognition technology, in place.
Clients surveyed indicate they want a faster and simpler passport application
process that includes the option to apply for a passport online. The use of
facial recognition technology supports automated processing to meet
increasing service demand and will enable eligible clients to apply to renew
their passport online.
3. Please describe any public benefits the system is expected to have.
The Program's use of facial recognition technology gives the Canada Border
Services Agency and other government agencies assurance that the travel
document holder is the owner of the identity presented and that they hold
Canadian citizenship, or Refugee, Protected Person, or Permanent Resident
status in Canada. The system helps support an online service channel option
and automated processing making the application process faster and simpler
for most clients.
4. How effective will the system likely be in meeting client needs?
Very effective [ Points: +0 ]
5. Please describe any improvements, benefits, or advantages you expect from using an
automated system. This could include relevant program indicators and performance targets.
The facial recognition system has enabled the Passport Program to advance
modernization initiatives while maintaining program integrity. This includes a
simplified renewal application process and automated decision-making for
routine non-complex applications that result in a positive decision. (i.e.
approximately 80% of adult passport renewals).
6. Please describe how you will ensure that the system is confined to addressing the client
needs identified above.
Access to the system is controlled and limited to use by IRCC's Passport
Program to help make determinations about a persons current, ongoing, and
future entitlement to a Canadian travel document.
7. Please describe any trade-offs between client interests and program objectives that you have
considered during the design of the project.
It is possible that the use of facial biometrics may engage a clients
reasonable expectation of privacy. With this in mind, the program completed
the following four-part test, as recommended by the Office of the Privacy
Commission, to assess whether the use of facial recognition technology is
justifiable when weighed against the prospective privacy concern.
Test 1. Is the measure demonstrably necessary to meet a specific need? Yes,
the Passport Program is mandated with issuing secure globally respected
travel documents to facilitate domestic and international travel. The strength
of the passport rests on the security of the document itself and the integrity of
passport entitlement processes. Use of facial recognition helps support
sound identity assessments which are essential to meet the objective of only
issuing passports to those who are eligible
Test 2. Is it likely to be effective in meeting that need? Yes, the facial
recognition system effectively meets the need to support sound identity and
entitlement assessments. Performance tests of the system has showed the
technology exhibited a high degree of accuracy by correctly matching pairs of
faces of the same person.
Test 3. Would the loss of privacy be proportionate to the benefit gained? Yes.
The conversion of a photo into a biometric template for facial recognition
comparison allows us to have more confidence in the identity of our clients,
which allows us to offer convenient service options like the simplified renewal
application process and will enable eligible clients to renew their passport
online. The use of facial recognition strengthens identity management
processes and helps protect client identities from being used by another
person to obtain a passport.
Test 4. Is there a less privacy-invasive way of achieving the same end? No.
While employees are trained to visually compare the likeness of applicants to
their previous passport photos (one-to-one verification), it is impossible for
them to compare each applicants face to all other applicant faces (one-to-
many identification). Studies also show that facial recognition technology, is
not only faster, but also superior to humans at face matching.
The biometrics community recogni
_full_text:
Algorithmic Impact Assessment Results
Version: 0.10.0
Project Details
1. Name of Respondent
Sean McNair
2. Job Title
Director General
3. Department
Citizenship and Immigration (Department of)
4. Branch
Visitors and Identity Management Branch
5. Project Title
Passport Program Facial Recognition System
6. Project ID from IT Plan
N/A
7. Departmental Program (from Department Results Framework)
Passport Program
8. Project Phase
Implementation [ Points: 0 ]
9. Please provide a project description:
The Passport Program (formerly Passport Canada) began using facial
recognition technology in 2010 to support identity authentication, help detect
fraud, and prevent passport and travel document issuance to ineligible
applicants.
Facial recognition technology is a tool that detects and analyzes the facial
features of a person’s face for the purpose of assisting in the identification
and verification of an individual’s identity. A facial recognition system converts
facial images into facial biometric templates that may be stored within the
system and used for future comparison. The system can compare a facial
biometric against a collection of other facial biometrics resulting in a list of
candidates ranked by a computer evaluated similarity score; this is called a
one-to-many (1:N) identification. It can also conduct a verification by
comparing a facial biometric to another facial biometric in the same claimed
identity; this is called a one-to-one (1:1) verification.
For passport purposes, three comparisons are performed. First, all adult
applicant facial biometrics are compared to a database of biometric
templates from past passport applicants to determine if the person has
previously applied for a passport and to help detect passport and travel
document related fraud. Similarly all adult applicants are compared to a
collection of templates of individuals whose entitlement to a Canadian
passport or travel document warrants additional review (e.g. individuals who
have previously misrepresented their identity). Finally, for returning applicants,
the Passport Program's facial recognition system compares the applicant's
facial biometric to previous templates in the same identity (i.e. previous
passport records) to verify they are the same person.
About The System
10. Please check which of the following capabilities apply to your system.
Image and object recognition: Analyzing very large data sets to automate the
recognition, classification, and context associated with an image or object.
Section 1: Impact Level : 2
Current Score: 42
Raw Impact Score: 42
Mitigation Score: 36
Section 2: Requirements Specific to Impact Level 2
Peer review
Consult at least one of the following experts and publish the complete review or a plain language
summary of the findings on a Government of Canada website:
qualified expert from a federal, provincial, territorial or municipal government institution
qualified members of faculty of a post-secondary institution
qualified researchers from a relevant non-governmental organization
contracted third-party vendor with a relevant specialization
a data and automation advisory board specified by Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat.
OR
Publish specifications of the automated decision system in a peer-reviewed journal. Where
access to the published review is restricted, ensure that a plain language summary of the
findings is openly available.
Gender-based Analysis Plus
Ensure that the Gender-based Analysis Plus addresses the following issues:
impacts of the automation project (including the system, data and decision) on gender and/or
other identity factors;
planned or existing measures to address risks identified through the Gender-based Analysis
Plus.
Notice
Plain language notice posted through all service delivery channels in use (Internet, in person,
mail or telephone).
Human-in-the-loop for decisions
Decisions may be rendered without direct human involvement.
Explanation
In addition to any applicable legal requirement, ensure that a meaningful explanation is provided
to the client with any decision that results in the denial of a benefit or service, or involves a
regulatory action. The explanation must inform the client in plain language of:
the role of the system in the decision-making process;
the training and client data, their source, and method of collection, as applicable;
the criteria used to evaluate client data and the operations applied to process it;
the output produced by the system and any relevant information needed to interpret it in the
context of the administrative decision; and
a justification of the administrative decision, including the principal factors that led to it.
Explanations must also inform clients of relevant recourse options, where appropriate.
A general description of these elements must also be made available through the Algorithmic
Impact Assessment and discoverable via a departmental website.
Training
Documentation on the design and functionality of the system.
IT and business continuity management
None
Approval for the system to operate
None
Other requirements
The Directive on Automated Decision-Making also includes other requirements that must be met
for all impact levels.
Link to the Directive on Automated Decision-Making
Contact your institution's ATIP office to discuss the requirement for a Privacy Impact
Assessment as per the Directive on Privacy Impact Assessment.
Section 3: Questions and Answers
Section 3.1: Impact Questions and Answers
Reasons for Automation
1. What is motivating your team to introduce automation into this decision-making process?
(Check all that apply)
Improve overall quality of decisions
Lower transaction costs of an existing program
The system is performing tasks that humans could not accomplish in a
reasonable period of time
Use innovative approaches
2. What client needs will the system address and how will this system meet them? If possible,
describe how client needs have been identified.
Clients want a secure, globally respected travel document that will facilitate
domestic and international travel. Canadian passport holders enjoy visa-free
access to most countries due, in part, to Canada's reputation of having strong
control measures, like the use of facial recognition technology, in place.
Clients surveyed indicate they want a faster and simpler passport application
process that includes the option to apply for a passport online. The use of
facial recognition technology supports automated processing to meet
increasing service demand and will enable eligible clients to apply to renew
their passport online.
3. Please describe any public benefits the system is expected to have.
The Program's use of facial recognition technology gives the Canada Border
Services Agency and other government agencies assurance that the travel
document holder is the owner of the identity presented and that they hold
Canadian citizenship, or Refugee, Protected Person, or Permanent Resident
status in Canada. The system helps support an online service channel option
and automated processing making the application process faster and simpler
for most clients.
4. How effective will the system likely be in meeting client needs?
Very effective [ Points: +0 ]
5. Please describe any improvements, benefits, or advantages you expect from using an
automated system. This could include relevant program indicators and performance targets.
The facial recognition system has enabled the Passport Program to advance
modernization initiatives while maintaining program integrity. This includes a
simplified renewal application process and automated decision-making for
routine non-complex applications that result in a positive decision. (i.e.
approximately 80% of adult passport renewals).
6. Please describe how you will ensure that the system is confined to addressing the client
needs identified above.
Access to the system is controlled and limited to use by IRCC's Passport
Program to help make determinations about a persons current, ongoing, and
future entitlement to a Canadian travel document.
7. Please describe any trade-offs between client interests and program objectives that you have
considered during the design of the project.
It is possible that the use of facial biometrics may engage a clients
reasonable expectation of privacy. With this in mind, the program completed
the following four-part test, as recommended by the Office of the Privacy
Commission, to assess whether the use of facial recognition technology is
justifiable when weighed against the prospective privacy concern.
Test 1. Is the measure demonstrably necessary to meet a specific need? Yes,
the Passport Program is mandated with issuing secure globally respected
travel documents to facilitate domestic and international travel. The strength
of the passport rests on the security of the document itself and the integrity of
passport entitlement processes. Use of facial recognition helps support
sound identity assessments which are essential to meet the objective of only
issuing passports to those who are eligible
Test 2. Is it likely to be effective in meeting that need? Yes, the facial
recognition system effectively meets the need to support sound identity and
entitlement assessments. Performance tests of the system has showed the
technology exhibited a high degree of accuracy by correctly matching pairs of
faces of the same person.
Test 3. Would the loss of privacy be proportionate to the benefit gained? Yes.
The conversion of a photo into a biometric template for facial recognition
comparison allows us to have more confidence in the identity of our clients,
which allows us to offer convenient service options like the simplified renewal
application process and will enable eligible clients to renew their passport
online. The use of facial recognition strengthens identity management
processes and helps protect client identities from being used by another
person to obtain a passport.
Test 4. Is there a less privacy-invasive way of achieving the same end? No.
While employees are trained to visually compare the likeness of applicants to
their previous passport photos (one-to-one verification), it is impossible for
them to compare each applicants face to all other applicant faces (one-to-
many identification). Studies also show that facial recognition technology, is
not only faster, but also superior to humans at face matching.
The biometrics community recognizes that the performance of facial
recognition technology can vary depending on factors like image quality, age,
gender and skin tone. Using high quality images along with a high performing
algorithm improves accuracy in general and across diverse populations.
IRCC's Passport Program requires that photos are taken by a commercial
photographer and must meet international standards. The Program also uses
an algorithm that has proven to perform well when tested by the National
Institute for Standard in Technology (NIST). False matches that may be
produced by the system are quickly adjudicated and returned to regular
processing within established service standards.
8. Have alternative non-automated processes been considered?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
9. If non-automated processes were considered, why was automation identified as the preferred
option?
A non-automated process may be completed by humans who visually
compare the client's passport photo to photos associated to previous
applications in the same identity to assess their similarity. The facial
recognition system can complete this comparison (one-to-one verification)
and can also compare the clients face to other clients faces to assess their
similarity and help flag possible instances of client's who are already known
by another identity (one-to-many identification). The latter comparison is not
feasible using a non-automated process.
10. What would be the consequence of not deploying the system?
Service cannot be delivered in a timely or efficient manner [ Points: +2 ]
Service quality is not as high [ Points: 0 ]
Other (please specify) [ Points: 0 ]
11. Please describe
Integrity of the passport issuance process is not as high.
Risk Profile
12. Is the project within an area of intense public scrutiny (e.g. because of privacy concerns)
and/or frequent litigation?
Yes [ Points: +3 ]
13. Are clients in this line of business particularly vulnerable?
No [ Points: +0 ]
14. Are stakes of the decisions very high?
No [ Points: +0 ]
15. Will this project have major impacts on staff, either in terms of their numbers or their roles?
No [ Points: +0 ]
16. Will the use of the system create or exacerbate barriers for persons with disabilities?
No [ Points: +0 ]
Project Authority
17. Will you require new policy authority for this project?
No [ Points: +0 ]
About the Algorithm
18. The algorithm used will be a (trade) secret
Yes [ Points: +3 ]
19. The algorithmic process will be difficult to interpret or to explain
No [ Points: +0 ]
About the Decision
20. Please describe the decision(s) that will be automated.
The system compares the client's facial biometric to previous facial biometric
templates in the same identity to assess whether the client appears to be
same person who applied for passport services in this identity before to help
verify they are who they say they are. The system also compares the likeness
of the clients facial biometric to facial biometrics associated to other
identities that are held in the facial recognition system to assess whether the
client may be known to the Passport Program under another identity. If the
system determines that the client appears to be the same person who used
this identity before and they do not appear to be known by another identity, the
application is automatically returned to continue processing. Facial
recognition is just one of many checks that are completed as part of the
passport issuance process and facial recognition results are never the sole
factor that determines whether a client may be issued a passport or travel
document.
21. Does the decision pertain to any of the categories below (check all that apply):
Other (please specify) [ Points: +1 ]
22. Please describe
Canadian passport and travel document services
Impact Assessment
23. Which of the following best describes the type of automation you are planning?
Partial automation (the system will contribute to administrative decision-
making by supporting an officer through assessments, recommendations,
intermediate decisions, or other outputs) [ Points: +2 ]
24. Please describe the role of the system in the decision-making process.
The system plays a role in identity and entitlement assessments by converting
passport photos into facial biometrics and comparing them to other facial
biometrics held in the facial recognition system. These comparisons help to
determine whether the client may have previously applied for a passport in the
same identity or another identity, and whether their entitlement to a passport
may warrant further review. The system produces a numeric value that
represents the similarity score of the facial biometrics being compared (i.e.
how similar or dissimilar are the faces). The score either falls within (pass) or
outside (fail) a set threshold that is also represented by a numeric value. Only
cases that pass all facial recognition comparison checks may be fully
automated. All cases that fail a facial recognition comparison are referred to
employees for assessment and decision. The system is just one of many
integrity tools in place and does not render a final decision to approve or
refuse passport issuance or to revoke a passport.
25. Will the system be making decisions or assessments that require judgement or discretion?
No [ Points: +0 ]
26. Please describe the criteria used to evaluate client data and the operations applied to
process it.
Client photos are the primary data source. Passport photos must be taken by
a commercial photographer within 6 months of applying for a passport and
must capture the person's neutral facial expression. Only photos that meet
specifications based on international standards are acceptable. The
combination of these requirements (recent, high quality, standardized)
minimizes the risk that outdated or unreliable data is used. Passport officers
are trained to review the photo for acceptability and the facial recognition
system has a built in feature to assess photo compatibility.
27. Please describe the output produced by the system and any relevant information needed to
interpret it in the context of the administrative decision.
The system assigns a numeric score to reflect how similar or dissimilar two
faces are. Scores that meet a set similarity threshold, as represented by a
numeric value, are automatically passed and returned to processing. A score
that does not meet a set similarity threshold is referred for human review and
decision. As part of the first review, the human operator is shown the two
faces in question and asked to decide if they depict the same person or a
different person. The system produced score is not shown to the operator to
avoid adding bias to their decision. This operator is unaware of which type of
comparison generated the failed case and does not know whether the two
faces are supposed to be the same person or a different person. If the
operator independently reaches the same decision as the system, the case is
automatically escalated to a higher level of review where operators are able to
access the system generated similarity score and information that would
appear on the associated travel document/application (personalization and
issuance details) to help adjudicate the case. If the next operator agrees that
the case is problematic it is escalated to another operator for review. The
facial recognition system never makes a decision to issue or refuse to issue a
travel document. All decisions to refuse to issue a travel document, withhold
service or revoke a travel document are made by a human and never based
solely on the result of a facial recognition comparison result.
28. Will the system perform an assessment or other operation that would not otherwise be
completed by a human?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
29. If yes, please describe the relevant function(s) of the system.
The system uses a different method than humans to compare faces; facial
biometric versus visual inspection. The system also compares the client's
face to millions of biometrics in other identities (e.g. all other adult passport
applicants) which cannot be completed by a human.
30. Is the system used by a different part of the organization than the ones who developed it?
No [ Points: +0 ]
31. Are the impacts resulting from the decision reversible?
Reversible [ Points: +1 ]
32. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
Impacts can last years. While the facial recognition system does not render a
decision to issue or not issue a passport, its decision is one component that
feeds into automated approval processes. The impact of a decision to issue a
passport can last years given passports are valid for up to ten years and may
be eligible for renewal.
33. The impacts that the decision will have on the rights or freedoms of individuals will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
34. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The system does not make a decision to issue or refuse to issue a travel
document and therefore its decision will have little to no impact on the rights
or freedoms of individuals. All decisions to refuse to issue a travel document,
withhold service or revoke a travel document are made by a human and never
based solely on the result of a facial recognition comparison result.
35. The impacts that the decision will have on the equality, dignity, privacy, and autonomy of
individuals will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
36. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The performance of facial recognition technology can exhibit some degree of
variability based on demographic factors such as gender, age, and skin tone.
As a result certain demographics may produce a higher false match rate than
others. However this will likely have little to no impact on the equality, dignity,
privacy, and autonomy of individuals because false matches are quickly
resolved and do not impact service standards. In addition, all decisions to
refuse to issue a travel document, withhold service or revoke a travel
document are made by a human and never based solely on the result of a
facial recognition comparison result.
37. The impacts that the decision will have on the health and well-being of individuals will likely
be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
38. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The system does not make a decision to issue or refuse to issue a travel
document and therefore its decisions will have little to no impact on the health
and well-being of individuals.
39. The impacts that the decision will have on the economic interests of individuals will likely
be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
40. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The system does not make a decision to issue or refuse to issue a travel
document and therefore its decisions will have little to no impact on the
economic interests of individuals.
41. The impacts that the decision will have on the ongoing sustainability of an environmental
ecosystem, will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
42. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The system does not make a decision to issue or refuse to issue a travel
document, therefore its decisions will not have an impact on environmental
sustainability.
About the Data - A. Data Source
43. Will the Automated Decision System use personal information as input data?
Yes [ Points: +4 ]
44. Have you verified that the use of personal information is limited to only what is directly
related to delivering a program or service?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
45. Is the personal information of individuals being used in a decision-making process that
directly affects those individuals?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
46. Have you verified if the system is using personal information in a way that is consistent
with: (a) the current Personal Information Banks (PIBs) and Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs)
of your programs or (b) planned or implemented modifications to the PIBs or PIAs that take new
uses and processes into account?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
47. Please list relevant PIB Bank Numbers.
PPU 080 Certificates of Identity and Refugee Travel Documents; PPU 081
Regular and Official Passports; PPU 082 Security and Intelligence Case
Management
48. What is the highest security classification of the input data used by the system? (Select one)
Protected B / Protected C [ Points: +3 ]
49. Who controls the data?
Federal government [ Points: +1 ]
50. Will the system use data from multiple different sources?
Yes [ Points: +4 ]
51. Will the system require input data from an Internet- or telephony-connected device? (e.g.
Internet of Things, sensor)
No [ Points: +0 ]
52. Will the system interface with other IT systems?
Yes [ Points: +4 ]
53. Who collected the data used for training the system?
A foreign government or non-government third party [ Points: +4 ]
54. Who collected the input data used by the system?
Your institution [ Points: +1 ]
55. Please describe the input data collected and used by the system, its source, and method of
collection.
The input data collected by the system comes from client's passport and
travel document applications. The information is typed and scanned into the
passport issuance systems. The client's photo and biographic information is
extracted and shared with the facial recognition system. Client's photos are
converted into biometric templates and stored in the system's photo galleries.
On a case by case basis, when there is a clear and direct link to administering
the Passport Program's mandate, the Program may also collect biographic
information and photos from intelligence and security partners on individuals
whose entitlement to a travel document may warrant further review. These
photos are converted into biometric templates and may be stored in the facial
recognition system.
About the Data - B. Type of Data
56. Will the system require the analysis of unstructured data to render a recommendation or a
decision?
No [ Points: 0 ]
Section 3.2: Mitigation Questions and Answers
Consultations
1. Internal Stakeholders (federal institutions, including the federal public service)
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
2. Which Internal Stakeholders have you engaged?
Strategic Policy and Planning
Program Policy
Legal Services
Access to Information and Privacy Office
Communications services
Digital Policy
Other (describe)
3. Please describe
Equity and GBA Plus
4. External Stakeholders (groups in other sectors or jurisdictions)
No [ Points: +0 ]
De-Risking and Mitigation Measures - Data Quality
5. Do you have documented processes in place to test datasets against biases and other
unexpected outcomes? This could include experience in applying frameworks, methods,
guidelines or other assessment tools.
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
6. Is this information publicly available?
No [ Points: +0 ]
7. Have you developed a process to document how data quality issues were resolved during the
design process?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
8. Is this information publicly available?
No [ Points: +0 ]
9. Have you undertaken a Gender Based Analysis Plus of the data?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
10. Is this information publicly available?
No [ Points: +0 ]
11. Have you assigned accountability in your institution for the design, development,
maintenance, and improvement of the system?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
12. Do you have a documented process to manage the risk that outdated or unreliable data is
used to make an automated decision?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
13. Is this information publicly available?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
14. Is the data used for this system posted on the Open Government Portal?
No [ Points: +0 ]
De-Risking and Mitigation Measures - Procedural
Fairness
15. Does the audit trail identify the authority or delegated authority identified in legislation?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
16. Does the system provide an audit trail that records all the recommendations or decisions
made by the system?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
17. Are all key decision points identifiable in the audit trail?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
18. Are all key decision points within the automated system's logic linked to the relevant
legislation, policy or procedures?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
19. Do you maintain a current and up to date log detailing all of the changes made to the model
and the system?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
20. Does the system's audit trail indicate all of the decision points made by the system?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
21. Can the audit trail generated by the system be used to help generate a notification of the
decision (including a statement of reasons or other notifications) where required?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
22. Does the audit trail identify precisely which version of the system was used for each
decision it supports?
No [ Points: +0 ]
23. Does the audit trail show who an authorized decision-maker is?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
24. Is the system able to produce reasons for its decisions or recommendations when required?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
25. Is there a process in place to grant, monitor, and revoke access permission to the system?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
26. Is there a mechanism to capture feedback by users of the system?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
27. Is there a recourse process established for clients that wish to challenge the decision?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
28. Does the system enable human override of system decisions?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
29. Is there a process in place to log the instances when overrides were performed?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
30. Does the system's audit trail include change control processes to record modifications to the
system's operation or performance?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
31. Have you prepared a concept case to the Government of Canada Enterprise Architecture
Review Board?
No [ Points: +0 ]
De-Risking and Mitigation Measures - Privacy
32. If your system uses or creates personal information, have you undertaken a Privacy Impact
Assessment, or updated an existing one?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
33. Please indicate the following in your answer: Title and scope of the Privacy Impact
Assessment; How the automation project fits into the program; and Date of Privacy Impact
Assessment completion or modification.
In 2003 Passport Canada completed a privacy impact assessment (Facial
Recognition Project Privacy Impact Assessment) that assessed privacy
issues and risks associated with the proof of concept of using facial
recognition to compare (mock up) passport photos against photos supplied
by three stakeholder departments (Royal Canadian Mounted Police,
Citizenship and Immigration Canada, and Canadian Security and Intelligence
Service). In 2011, Passport Canada completed a second assessment (Facial
Recognition Project Privacy Impact Assessment) which assessed privacy
risks associated to the Passport Program's use of as facial recognition
system as part of the passport entitlement process. In 2024-25, Immigration,
Refugees, and Citizenship Canada's Passport Program (formerly Passport
Canada) will complete a new privacy impact assessment of the Passport
Program's Facial Recognition System to assess privacy risks associated to
changes to the existing system.
34. Have you designed and built security and privacy into your systems from the concept stage
of the project?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
35. Is the information used within a closed system (i.e. no connections to the Internet, Intranet
or any other system)?
No [ Points: +0 ]
36. If the sharing of personal information is involved, has an agreement or arrangement with
appropriate safeguards been established?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
37. Will you de-identify any personal information used or created by the system at any point in
the lifecycle?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
38. Please describe your de-identification method(s).
For research and development activities, data may be depersonalized and a
unique identifier could be used instead. (e.g., performance testing)
